item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: Whacked out Wednesday memes or whatever I don’t know, just enjoy
item.description:
Welcome to this dump of random memes. Sometimes I make them themed, sometimes I don't. If you enjoy laughing without talking politics, getting away from the horrors of the world, or escaping reality, you’ll love following this page!
I have to tell every door dasher that so they can find my house because Google is wrong
Amen
Boomer humor is still alive at Disney
Had a boss that watched porn at work and we all knew it. He didn’t ever get fired.
Time for another trip to HR
When I was a kid, the adult store said Christie’s Toy Box so I thought it had toys. Was so annoyed that my parents wouldn’t take me there. I went as an adult. Boring. I want real toys!
Diabolical
Creative thinker
I hope that’s not his real head
Too bad. Tummy wants its happy food.
He all mad
I’ll bet the hostess laughed her butt off
That’s…interesting
Yes, I’ll also have water
This is why I don’t eat crunchy people’s food
Is there even tea in the cup?
It’s probably got more followers than you
Had he grown into an adult during the coma?
Notice the sarcasm
The rest of the men out the rest of the French?
I want all of there
Trash panda
Sometimes I wonder if the writers DID know these were dirty and just laughed knowing the innocent comic readers wouldn’t get it
Oops, it’s too high
How I adult
Always buy the treat
The Internet isn’t the place to look for that
Where’s the fan?
But did it help your headache?
Important things to consider in a relationship
Should get a fake one, put it on, and rub it off in front of them
I’m totally not a virgin
Want this for my house
No seatbelt?
Wow
Took me a minute to realize he was talking about the girl
I don’t understand doctors.
Um plot twist: it didn’t work
Don’t do that in the South
Me too
Yup
Someone was smart
Yup
My son
Worst
Heck yeah
Comfort is key
Heck yeah
Every time

item.title: FAFO :)
item.description: morbidtruth In 2019, a couple from Visalia—Corey Cornutt, 25, and Savannah Grillot, 29—were accused of repeatedly setting traps for suspected thieves after experiencing multiple break-ins to their own vehicles. According to investigators, the pair left an unlocked bicycle in their front yard as bait, then waited for someone to attempt to take it. When a person approached or tried to steal the bike, the couple allegedly rushed out armed with aluminum baseball bats and attacked them. Police said the incidents occurred between July and November 2019 and that the couple recorded the confrontations on video before posting them to YouTube and Facebook. Authorities identified at least four victims, including one who required hospital treatment for non-life-threatening injuries. Neighbors reported frequently finding blood on nearby streets and sidewalks after the alleged ambushes, and investigators noted that the couple never contacted law enforcement to report the attempted thefts. In January 2020, the pair were arrested and charged with assault with a deadly weapon and conspiracy. However, later legal developments revealed that local prosecutors ultimately declined to pursue the case, and the couple was cleared of criminal wrongdoing. https://www.instagram.com/morbidtruth/ https://www.wric.com/news/u-s-world/couple-left-bikes-in-yard-to-lure-thieves-beat-them-with-bats-and-post-videos-to-youtube-police-say/

item.title: Consider sharing this. Maybe this'll make sense for the clueless. Oh, and I'm bracing for the downvote storm ahead! INCOMING!!
item.description: Sec. of Agriculture, Brooke Rollins, disavowed responsibility for the current screwworm outbreak, which the US had eradicated. This parasite affects livestock (like cattle and sheep) and wildlife (like deer). Outbreaks can create significant economic burdens due to lost livestock, higher beef prices, expensive medical treatments, and tighter regulations on agricultural trade. https://www.youtube.com/shorts/qRJYTGiUtzQ        'DOGE' cut programs and funding willy-nilly with no research or in-depth analysis... who could have ever predicted something like this? /s        What will come from these, and all the other, cuts:        ➤ Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA): Large cuts in funding and personnel. These actions have significantly reduced the agency's operational capacity and disaster response abilities. The administration continues to push for disaster relief responsibilities to be handled increasingly at the state level.        ➤ U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID): Placed 4,700 workers on leave and terminated 1,600 employees. Eliminated USAID’s health statistics reporting systems across 90 developing countries, which severely cripples global early-warning networks for tracking new pandemic outbreaks. Also, public health organizations estimate that stopping USAID's global health initiatives, humanitarian aid, and disaster relief could lead to 740,000 preventable deaths globally, mostly among children.        ➤ National Institutes of Health (NIH): Cut $4 billion in funding that was focused on medical research. This froze or canceled critical grants distributed to American universities, hospitals, and specialized research institutes. The abrupt loss of capital has stalled long-term biomedical research dedicated to finding cures and treatments for life-threatening conditions such as cancer, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases.        ➤ Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC): Massive personnel cuts across the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), including laying off 2,400 workers (18%) at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).        ➤ Head Start and the Office of Child Care: Cut 40% to 50% of the staff and closed half of the regional offices. This has caused funding delays and interruptions which affect preschools.        ➤ Department of Education: Laid off 1,300 workers, which has severely disrupted the federal administration of student financial aid, civil rights enforcement in schools, and structural support programs designed for underprivileged students and children with disabilities.        ➤ Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB): Now operating in a severely reduced capacity. The Trump administration and Acting Director Russell Vought have made repeated attempts to defund, lay off staff, and close the independent agency. However, federal judges have blocked these efforts, stating that closing a Congressionally mandated agency requires an act of Congress.        ➤ IRS: Cut 20% of the workforce, crippling its capacity to audit complex corporate structures and wealthy tax evaders. Treasury officials estimate that this loss of enforcement capability will cause a 10% drop in overall tax revenues.        Not the complete list, folks.

26 Man Tax: Aaron Perilo
"Genius is perseverance in disguise." - Mike Newlin So, I made a discord server. It's a relaxed environment where you can take/donate memes to the cause, play trivia twice a week, chat about lots of topics or about nothing at all. We also have Jack Box game night and are setting up movie night! Very laid back/relaxed vibe. Come hang with us. https://discord.gg/AWFV7FT3RQ I'm also on the imgcat server for updates & fun: https://discord.gg/TpjD37jgJ9 https://ko-fi.com/mememuseumcurator Unable to work, zero income. Feel free to leave me a tip. Those that have, y'all are the bee's knees. You've honestly been my lifeline. For the couple people that have said I'm "scamming" people, I only get SNAP, so this money goes to things like TP, shampoo, laundry detergent, etc. Tag me if you see an artist/creator you like whose work isn't getting seen as much as it should be! Or if you are an artist/creator and want me to post something of yours, **Imgur Creator Spotlight is now in your hands!! Having trouble finding people I haven't already featured recently. Now I'm going to just do who YOU nominate. Tag me or DM me and I'll feature whoever you pick.*

item.dc:creator: DropDrop

item.description: Before this, he could not breathe properly. Crouzon syndrome is a rare genetic condition affecting about 1 in 60,000 births. The bones in the skull fuse too early, a process called craniosynostosis. When those bones stop expanding at the right time, the face can not grow forward the way it should. The midface stays underdeveloped. The eye sockets remain shallow, causing the eyes to bulge outward. The airways do not open up enough, leading to obstructive sleep apnea and serious breathing problems. This child underwent Le Fort III distraction osteogenesis, a surgical procedure that gradually moves the entire midface forward. The device you see is called a Rigid External Distraction (RED) frame. Surgeons attach it to the skull with pins, then make precise cuts in the facial bones. Over the course of about two to three weeks, the device is adjusted daily to pull the midface forward, typically 1mm per day. The bones slowly separate and new bone fills in the gaps. The frame stays on for roughly three months total to allow the bones to consolidate and heal in their new position. The results can be dramatic. The eyes sit back into properly sized orbits instead of bulging dangerously forward. The airways open, allowing the child to breathe without obstruction. Sleep apnea improves or resolves. Vision stabilizes. The facial structure becomes more balanced. For many children with Crouzon syndrome, this surgery is lifesaving. Without it, increased pressure on the brain, permanent vision loss, and chronic breathing failure are real risks.

item.title: Nothing to lose but your chains
item.description:
Nothing out of the ordinary, regularly scheduled pay bump. I'm happy to have it.
But I can't help but sit here and think, that this one raise is more than what the federal government deems our lives to be worth. Everybody else knows your worth, they're just hoping you don't.

item.title: GovBrief Today #496
item.description: Alabama doesn't need nitrogen gas to execute Jeffery Lee tonight, but keeps fighting to use it. The federal judge who ruled the nitrogen method cruel and unusual left two other methods available. Alabama appealed for the right to use the gas and lost again Wednesday night. Now the state is asking the Supreme Court. Lee's jury voted for life imprisonment almost thirty years ago, but the trial judge overruled them using a practice the state later abolished. Veterinary guidelines reject using this gas on most animals unless they are sedated first, and Alabama's protocol includes no sedative. Alabama already has the right to execute Lee tonight. What it's fighting for is the chance to do it in a way your local vet wouldn't. I've emailed 7,766 stories over 496 straight nights, free and without ads. The algorithm can't show them all to you. Add your email address at the website and I will. #GovBriefToday #Resist Subscribe (It's free!): https://gbounacos.substack.com/ Today's full brief: https://go.govbrief.today/260610-full [1] https://go.govbrief.today/iran-war-june10 [2] https://go.govbrief.today/rollins-screwworm [3] https://go.govbrief.today/gates-house [4] https://go.govbrief.today/pipe-break [5] https://go.govbrief.today/fla-maps-upheld [6] https://go.govbrief.today/inflation-rises [7] https://go.govbrief.today/trump-loves-inflation [8] https://go.govbrief.today/china-websites [9] https://go.govbrief.today/leon-warning [10] https://go.govbrief.today/nitrogen-appeal [11] https://go.govbrief.today/fbi-garden-grove [12] https://go.govbrief.today/johnson-cfpb [13] https://go.govbrief.today/trump-club-health Subscribe (It's free!): https://gbounacos.substack.com/
item.dc:creator: GeorgeBounacos

item.title: 1929 Smith Corona No.4 Restoration: Done!
item.description:
Okay, to call this a restoration is prooobably stretching the definition of the word... but I'm gonna use it anyway because language is fluid and I'm a corrupting influence! :D So! As a refresher, this is a 1929 Smith Corona No.4 that I picked up a couple years back while I was out cash register hunting. I put it on the back burner while I was tackling bigger projects and it sort of ended up always sitting nearby but never on the project table. Now that my area seems to be bone dry for reasonably priced cash registers, I've got some time to plug away at these oddball projects! As always, if you want to support me or just throw hot coffee at my face, I'm set up over here! https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse
So, the thing about manual typewriters is that they're pretty sensitive to dust and grit. That's why these portables especially are best stored in the travel boxes they originally came with, or inside an airtight bag if they're in storage so they don't gradually crud up and stop working. Because all the moving parts are so lightweight and rely on gravity or small springs to return to their resting position, it doesn't take a lot to make the keys stick. This typewriter's not in terrible condition by any stretch, but the faint browning on the keybed is a mixture of dust and cigarette oil that tends to get gluey very quickly. Some of the keys are sticky on this machine, so part of what I do when I start is to flush out the keybed with rubbing alcohol and canned air to remove as much loose gunk as I can. Because I am a very smart person, I completely whiffed photographing that part. Imagine it in your miiiinds...
Once the alcohol had evaporated out and the keys all felt pretty loose and mobile, I run a thin line of gun oil along the keybed and work each key manually to get some of that oil into the system. It only needs very little, most of it gets wiped up with a shop towel. Too much oil will make the keys stick just as easily. I just want enough inside those narrow slots to prevent rusting.
Once the sticky keys were finished up, I moved onto the keys themselves. Just like with cash register buttons, the keys on typewriters can get pretty gunky. I don't use any special chemicals on these. Just dish soap and water that I dip Q-Tips into. I let them soak into the grime for a couple minutes, then sweep it out with a clean Q-Tip. Literal rinse and repeat until the keys are reasonably clean. You don't want to go overboard on older typewriters since the lettering on the keys can be a cardboard print under clear plastic, which will trap moisture and make for an ugly key when it dries.
Thankfully, these keys were pretty easy work. With those done, I went over the painted portions of the typewriter with shop towels dampened with soapy water to pull off the nicotine oil, dirt, and dust. I have yet to own a typewriter that didn't originally come to me loaded with cigarette scum, and the majority of any cleaning is usually taken up trying to remove as much as I can get at. The tricky part is getting into all the awkward spots under and between the keys, under the ribbon holders, around the platen roll and carriage... really everywhere that isn't the front or sides. It took a lot of paper towels, a lot of q-tips, and a couple hours of a good audiobook before I was satisfied I'd gotten everything I could get to clean.
This little smudge stuck around once the paint dried, and it's in a problematic area. The paint it landed on is textured over the top of the green base layer, and I've seen these typewriters with that texture flaked off. I didn't want to overwork that one spot for that reason, so I had to go at it gently with soap and water, letting it dry enough to see what was left, and again with soap and water until it was as gone as I could confidently manage. Part of it, I think, is that whatever was there developed mold which affected the paint itself. In the later pictures you'll see a ghost of it, but it's really something you have to look for to notice. I call that a win. :)
The key slugs really didn't need much work at all! I used a dental wax-up pick to pull the bits of old ink out of the O's and D's where they like to get trapped, but other than that, these puppies were kept pretty clean by previous owners!
As for the underside, I gave it a very careful wiping down (careful not to disconnect or break any springs) after which I brushed a little gun oil around the joints of the moving parts to keep away any rust. We won't talk about the cloud of dust I blew out of there right before. :D
Now these little guys were interesting (at least to me!). They're the spools that feed inked ribbon through the typewriter, and they're oftentimes missing one or both spools. I suspect the black one is a replacement from a matching machine, and that's great to me. It means whoever had it last actually cared enough about keeping it functionally intact to track down a replacement!
They didn't require much cleaning, just some soap and water to loosen up the nicotine oil and old ink globs. Those silver clips are what hold onto the loose end of the ribbon, which beats the pants out of some of the less user friendly early versions that sported metal teeth.
Of course, you can't restore a 98 year old typewriter without modern car wax, right? ...right?
Look, sometimes you have to take a leap of faith and try something new! For posterity, here's the typewriter pre-wax, with that little blemish pretty visible.
And here it is post-wax! Honestly, I'm extremely happy this worked so well. The wax does a good job blending in the little blemishes, it leaves every surface feeling silky clean, and I feel like it really deepened the green.
I was even able to Q-Tip a bit of wax on the line ruler behind the ribbon vibrator (yes, that's what it's called). I had to be careful about this step because I didn't want to smear any wax under that mechanism, so it got applied and wiped clean in stages.
Of course, nothing is perfect. When the wax had enough time to completely dry, I found residue lurking around all those screw heads that needed to be cleaned up.
One clean and dry toothbrush later, and even the screws look new!
The back of the machine has seen some better days, but considering it's coming up on its centennial birthday, I think we can forgive some chipped paint. I'm just glad it's all concentrated on the back side!
While I was waxing, I took a little extra time to wipe down all the nickel with a clean Q-Tip just to make it all a bit shinier than it was before. Each key got pulled up, had the sides wiped relatively clean (can't go ham on them or they'll bend out of alignment), and I'm happy to call that job done.
It's hard to see, but that smudge is still faintly visible. Can't have everything be perfect, or else the next restoration won't live up to my inflated ego!
At this point, the only thing left to do is get some fresh ribbon in it and see how she works!
Thankfully, there's still people out there who sell new and custom typewriter ribbons. The site I use is Ribbons Unlimited, which is run by a couple out of their house. Fantastic people who've gone out of their way to help me find custom-sized ribbon for my super duper old registers.
In this case, I didn't want to just slap a couple plastic spools down. So I hacked the ends of the ribbon off and manually wound them onto the original metal spools. Fun fact, installing the ribbon upside-down is a rite of passage that I keep finding myself stumbling past even though I know there's only one correct orientation. I swear I might have poobrain.
Not pictured: me struggling to thread the ribbon through the very fiddly ribbon vibrator in the center.
Looking pretty good! Black is always on top, FYI. The only exception to that rule is all the exceptions to the rule I don't know about.
One thing worth mentioning about typewriter ribbon (look, I may have some form of autism for these things, sue me) is that the fabric of the ribbon matters. Cheaper ribbons are typically made from nylon, like the one I'm using here. They're very thin, prone to getting holes punched into them by the keys if they're used on an aged platen roll like the one on this machine, and the quality of the typing is generally lower overall. I like to use these less expensive ribbons to test typewriters out. I can cut the ribbon, make mistakes with it, and use them for display without worrying about the ink drying up. On a typewriter I'd actually want to use to write something out for, say, a framed page or a gift, it's better to use natural silk ribbon. They're a bit thicker, hold more ink, leave a more defined letter on the page, and they don't rip or tear nearly as easily. That, and they can be re-inked if you've got a single color. Anyhow, that's a lot more about ribbon than anyone needs to know, so let's move on!
Money shot! :D The platen roller is a little tough to get a good grip on the paper because it's so hardened with age, so I did have to give it a little help feeding it through. I *could* get the roller restored with new rubber, but the company who does it requires the roll be completely removed from the typewriter and they charge 100 bucks a roll plus shipping. On a typewriter that's pretty common and I don't intend to use daily... yeah, probably not! (Also taking apart the carriage to get at the roller is stu-pen-dously difficult and involves potentially breaking things)
And hey, only *slight* red/black misalignment on capital letters? That's pretty gosh diggity darn good for a machine that's been banged on since the (first) stock market collapse! Especially considering I'm typing on rock hard rubber! Also, for the keen-eyed among you, why yes there isn't a "1" key on this machine! There also isn't an "!" on it, either! To save space, the lowercase "L" substitutes as the number one and in order to type an exclamation point, you have to use an apostrophe, go back, and add a period to complete the symbol. Also-also, can we just appreciate the beauty that is that "%" sign? Augh. So good. So swoopy. Much style.
As always, the ding is necessary! (And yes, the ribbon vibrator is a *little* sticky and likes to hang out in the raise position. It's actually two pieces of spring steel crimped around one another with just enough play to let it rise and fall between key strikes. I'm pretty sure there's a bit of gunk still in that tiny space that I can't get to, and this is one of the mechanisms you absolutely do not add oil to. Since it's spring steel, unbending anything to clean it out would very likely break the metal and the mechanism, so this is something I'll have to keep flushing out with alcohol on occasion until it frees up completely. It just didn't want to play ball with me in time for this post.)
I mean, honestly. I can't remember the last time I had a *one day project.* One day! Start to finish, just a day! Hell, an afternoon! I've worked on the same restoration for MONTHS. I feel like I did nothing! Augh! This is why typewriters are evil. They're everywhere, they're affordable, and they multiply. If the cash register drought doesn't clear up soon, I'm going to have to go rummage saling for something different... <_<
(Artist: Zajice) Let me know what you think! And hey, if you've got suggestions on things I could have done better (I relish the advice of experienced nerds) or think of something I might want to try restoring, I'm open to new ideas! It basically depends on whether things are in my budget, actually for sale near me, and of course if it piques my fickle brain's neurons. :D Thank you as always, and if you want to help out with my hobby, here's where to go! https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: Diesel met his match: a catalytic converter.
item.dc:creator: tractorist
